successful_techniques: hotspot_targeting (cost-contribution scoring) + candidate_list_restricted k-opt moves (2opt/relocate/swap) + best_delta_selection with adaptive non-improving acceptance (stagnation-controlled)
cross_exchange(2–4 segment swap + optional reversal) + candidate_list_guidance(hotspot/nearest-neighbor restricted search) + delta_evaluation_with_budgeted_first_improvement
successful_patterns: Baseline randomly shuffles values at selected positions, breaking many good adjacencies. Candidate does cost-guided edge freezing (preserve low-cost edges) then greedily reconnects the remaining path

Baseline applies an uninformed random shuffle that often destroys good structure. The candidate finds a high-cost contiguous “bad” region, removes it, and reinserts (optionally reversed) into a few ta

The candidate replaces unguided random swaps with cost-directed, neighborhood-restricted micro k-opt moves (2-opt/relocate/swap) chosen by best delta and governed by an adaptive non-improvement accept

Candidate replaces exhaustive pairwise swaps with targeted large-neighborhood segment cross-exchange (2–4 node blocks), guided by worst local-cost “hot spots” and neighbor lists, using O(1) delta eval
Candidate replaces random VND steps with cost-aware relocate moves chosen by a global regret criterion and evaluated via deltas/candidate lists. This focuses search on high-impact nodes/positions and
failed_patterns: Candidate replaces simple random partial shuffle with cost-guided multi-block segment exchange plus occasional reversal. This injects large, correlated, structure-breaking changes (often 30–50% moved)

Candidate adds sampled ejection-chain (multi-step relocate) guided by neighbor lists and summed deltas. But it mixes stale indices with sequential relocations and uses noisy heuristics/random sampling
